chunk_summary: Defines moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in Indian clinics, emphasizing WHO criteria, operational definitions, and diagnostic pitfalls, including misclassification risks and measurement accuracy.
learning_objectives: Understand WHO diagnostic criteria for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children aged 6–59 months.
Identify operational definitions and safety-first approaches for MAM in Indian clinics.
Recognize common misclassification pitfalls, such as confusing underweight with MAM.
Learn practical bedside screening methods for wasting in outpatient settings.
key_facts: WHO defines MAM as weight-for-height/length (WHZ/WLZ) between -2 and -3 z-scores and/or MUAC 115–124 mm, with no bilateral pitting oedema.
Indian clinics use a safety-first definition: moderate wasting by WHO metrics, clinically stable, with appetite, and no acute danger signs.
Underweight (low weight-for-age) should not be equated with MAM, as it may indicate chronic stunting.
Check oedema by pressing both feet for several seconds and observing for persistent pitting.
MUAC values should be interpreted in millimeters to align with WHO thresholds; errors in manuals may include unit typos.
A one-minute wasting screen includes visible wasting, oedema check, MUAC measurement, and clinical history review.
reference_quote: WHO defines moderate wasting as weight-for-height/length (WHZ/WLZ) between -2 and -3 z-scores and/or MUAC 115–124 mm, with no bilateral pitting oedema. (Source: WHO, 2023)
document_key_topics: WHO criteria for moderate acute malnutrition
Indian clinic-specific MAM definitions and safety protocols
